stories.headline: DOJ Sues Colorado Over In-State Tuition for Undocumented Students
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Justice filed suit Thursday in U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado, targeting the state's 2013 ASSET law, which allows undocumented students who graduated from Colorado high schools or lived in the state for at least a year to pay in-state tuition rates. The DOJ argues the law violates federal statute because the discounted rates are unavailable to out-of-state U.S. citizens, constituting preferential treatment for people in the country illegally.

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser's office declined to comment Thursday. Former Gov. John Hickenlooper, who signed the ASSET law in 2013, called the suit an effort to "reignite yesterday's culture wars" rather than address systemic immigration reform. Denver Mayor Mike Johnston, who co-sponsored the bill as a state senator, said he entered politics because of students who could not afford out-of-state tuition and vowed city and state leaders would fight the federal challenge.

The lawsuit is part of a broader Trump administration push against similar tuition policies in Texas and other states. Colorado's law covers not only in-state tuition but also in-state financial aid for qualifying undocumented students.

stories.headline: Immigration Advocates Sue Town of Hudson to Block New ICE Detention Center
stories.summary:
A coalition of faith-based advocates called Together Colorado, joined by a Weld County resident, filed suit Wednesday in Weld County District Court against the Town of Hudson and its Planning Commission, arguing the town must enforce its own land development code before the federal government can reopen a shuttered private prison as a 1,188-bed ICE detention center. The property, which the Alaska Department of Corrections used until 2013, is zoned industrial and does not permit correctional facilities without a conditional-use permit approved through a public process.

The lawsuit targets private prison operator GEO Group, which signed a five-year, half-billion-dollar contract with ICE to operate the facility — to be called the Big Horn Contract Detention Center — with a lease beginning Aug. 1. The agreement would nearly double ICE detention capacity in Colorado, supplementing GEO's existing Aurora facility. Hudson, roughly 30 miles northeast of Denver, has been the site of repeated community protests.

Hudson Town Manager Bryce Lange said the U.S. Constitution supersedes local zoning and that the town will focus on roads, public safety and water services rather than attempting to block federal immigration enforcement. Attorneys with the nonprofit Towards Justice argued local governments are "not helpless to act" and that Hudson granted GEO and ICE a special privilege by not requiring the standard permitting process.

stories.headline: Manny Rutinel's Deleted Reddit Posts Show Past Support for Animal Liberation Movement
stories.summary:
Colorado Sun reporting reveals that state Rep. Manny Rutinel, a Democrat running for the competitive 8th Congressional District seat, wrote posts on Reddit in 2017 voicing support for an "animal liberation" movement that would grant animals the same legal rights as humans and effectively prohibit their slaughter for food. The posts, since deleted, show Rutinel envisioning a future in which such legislation would eventually be adopted.

Rutinel, now 31, told the Colorado Sun those views belonged to a 22-year-old and no longer reflect his beliefs. He said he stopped being vegan several years ago and acknowledged he cannot pinpoint exactly when his thinking changed. He declined to directly answer whether he believed at the time that animals should not be slaughtered for food.

The revelation is politically sensitive given the geography of the 8th District, which stretches from Denver's northeast suburbs into Greeley and is home to ranches, feedlots, dairy farms, and the JBS meatpacking plant — one of the nation's largest beef slaughterhouses. Animal agriculture in the district generated roughly $1.2 billion in products in 2022. The 8th District is considered a toss-up seat in the upcoming midterm election.

stories.headline: BLM Lease Sales Prompt Alarm Over Colorado Public Lands, Water Sources
stories.summary:
Local elected officials are urging Coloradans to submit public comments before an Aug. 24 deadline on a proposed Bureau of Land Management rule that would reduce bonding requirements and fees for the oil and gas industry and curtail public review processes. The proposal follows the largest oil and gas lease sale in modern Colorado history last month, in which BLM auctioned more than 134,000 acres of public land, with a second sale of nearly 127,000 acres scheduled for December.

Two-thirds of the leased acreage lies just south of Dinosaur National Monument and overlaps critical winter range and migration corridors for mule deer, pronghorn, and the nation's largest elk herd. Other parcels are near community water supplies, including Aurora Reservoir, which serves approximately 400,000 residents. Roughly 5,000 acres are also on the Roan Plateau, which a 2014 legal settlement was intended to shield from leasing.

Federal law now requires BLM to hold at least four lease sales per year in Colorado and eight other Western states regardless of wildlife or environmental conflicts, and local land managers have lost the discretion to defer sensitive parcels. Officials say the false choice between energy development and environmental protection ignores the mix of land uses — including hunting, recreation, and agriculture — that underpin local economies across western Colorado.

stories.headline: Tap & Burger Closes Denver Tech Center Location, Citing Competition and Rising Costs
stories.summary:
Tap & Burger has permanently closed its Denver Tech Center restaurant at 4910 S. Newport St., ending an eight-year run at that location. Culinary Creative Group founder Juan Padró told BusinessDen that the DTC concept no longer aligns with the company's growth strategy, which is now focused on higher-end concepts including A5, Fox and Hen, Ash'kara and Aviano.

The closure follows a sustained sales decline after a competing bar concept, Peckish, opened in the same building in September 2024 — a move Padró said violated a noncompete clause in his lease. Sales at the DTC location had dropped from more than $3 million annually in peak years to a mid-$2 million pace in 2026. Padró said building owner Equity Residential worked with him to exit the lease with one year remaining after acknowledging the noncompete violation.

Padró cited rising minimum wages, rents and food costs as structural pressures making the burger-and-bar category increasingly unviable in Denver. He noted that a fairly priced Denver burger would now cost around $25, a figure consumers resist. The LoHi and Westminster Tap & Burger locations will remain open.

stories.headline: Northern Water Commits $30 Million Over 30 Years to Restore Grand Lake Water Clarity
stories.summary:
Northern Water will spend $1 million per year for 30 years — a total of $30 million — on testing and implementing solutions to restore clarity and water quality at Grand Lake, Colorado's deepest natural lake and a major tourist destination near Rocky Mountain National Park's west entrance. It is the first time a dedicated funding source has been established for the lake's restoration, according to Northern Water spokesperson Jeff Stahla.

The problem stems from the Colorado-Big Thompson water delivery system, which moves water from the Upper Colorado River through Lake Granby, Shadow Mountain Reservoir and Grand Lake before transporting it under the Continental Divide to serve more than 1 million Front Range residents and thousands of irrigated farmland acres. The transfer process carries algae and sediment into Grand Lake, causing blooms, weed growth and reduced clarity that have persisted for decades.

Efforts to fix the problem date to at least 2008, when the Colorado Water Quality Control Commission set a clarity standard. A federal environmental review launched in 2016 stalled in 2020 when the Bureau of Reclamation said it could not identify workable structural alternatives or funding. Colorado lawmakers passed a resolution in 2024 calling for renewed action, and the new funding agreement is being described as a turning point in the long-running dispute.

stories.headline: Denver Approves No-Cost Agreement for New Pedestrian Bridge Linking LoDo to Ball Arena
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council has approved an intergovernmental agreement allowing two Kroenke Sports and Entertainment metropolitan districts to finance and construct the Wynkoop Crossing, a pedestrian and bicycle bridge over Speer Boulevard connecting Lower Downtown to Ball Arena. The city bears no cost; the districts will own, finance and maintain the structure using a regional mill levy charged to property owners within the district.

The bridge is expected to be completed by April 29, 2029. Designed by Shears Adkins Rockmore Architects, it will use a looping ramp on the LoDo side for universal access without elevators, landing near Manny's Bridge and the Downtown Children's Playground before touching down at Chopper Circle on the arena's front entrance.

The crossing is a key piece of infrastructure for Kroenke's broader River Mile redevelopment of 55 acres around Ball Arena, which received city approval in 2024. The full project — expected to wrap around 2050 — envisions approximately 6,000 housing units (18% affordable), a new city park, a hotel, a potential new performance venue, and retail and restaurant space.

stories.headline: Colorado Sees 178 Cyclosporiasis Cases; State Tracking Uptick But No Local Outbreak Detected
stories.summary:
Colorado has recorded 178 cases of cyclosporiasis, the parasitic illness known for severe gastrointestinal symptoms, but state health officials say there is no indication of a local outbreak, according to the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment. More than 80 percent of completed case investigations — 136 of 154 — have been linked to international travel rather than domestic exposure.

The numbers put Colorado on pace to exceed its full-year 2025 total of 204 cases. CDPHE spokesperson Hope Shuler said four Coloradans have been hospitalized this year due to the illness. Five Colorado cases involved people who reported spending time in states at the center of a multistate outbreak — Michigan, Ohio, Indiana, West Virginia and Kentucky — but no common in-state sources of infection have been identified.

The national outbreak has been chaotic, with the CDC initially linking iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms to the surge before retracting a claim of a positive test while still naming the lettuce as a prime suspect. Taylor Farms has issued a voluntary recall of iceberg lettuce distributed to 27 states, a list that does not include Colorado. Michigan alone has reported more than 7,000 cases.

stories.headline: Fort Collins Man Sues City, Officers After Wrongful DUI Arrest Despite Negative Drug and Alcohol Tests
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old Colorado State University student has filed a lawsuit against the City of Fort Collins and two police officers over what he says was a wrongful DUI arrest. Evidence gathered after his arrest showed he had neither alcohol nor drugs in his system, according to the lawsuit.

Details of the specific circumstances leading to the arrest were not fully disclosed in available reporting, but the suit alleges officers lacked sufficient justification to make the arrest. The case adds to a growing body of civil litigation in Colorado and nationally challenging roadside impairment determinations made without chemical confirmation.

Fort Collins, roughly 65 miles north of Denver, is home to Colorado State University's main campus. The outcome of the lawsuit could have implications for how law enforcement in the region conducts DUI stops and the standard of evidence required before an arrest is made.

stories.headline: Two Denver Music Festivals — Underground Music Showcase and Blucifer's First Rodeo — Take Over RiNo and South Broadway This Weekend
stories.summary:
Denver's RiNo Arts District and South Broadway neighborhoods will host two back-to-back music festivals this weekend: the Underground Music Showcase, which relocated from South Broadway to RiNo last year, and Blucifer's First Rodeo, an artist-led festival that emerged to fill the void UMS left on South Broadway. Together the two events feature 429 bands spread across more than two dozen venues including breweries, galleries, diners and dive bars.

UMS weekend passes are priced at $95 and include access to headliners such as synthy-soul act 54 Ultra, indie duo slenderbodies and pop artist Goldie Boutilier. All-access passes to Blucifer's First Rodeo are sold out, but $20 all-ages satellite tickets remain available at D3 Arts, and the festival also includes invitation-only Sunday house party shows.

The dual-festival weekend marks a new chapter for both events as they settle into their respective new identities and venues.

stories.headline: Rapids Defender Lucas Herrington Reflects on World Cup Penalty Miss, Looks Ahead
stories.summary:
Colorado Rapids center back Lucas Herrington, 18, returned to Dick's Sporting Goods Park in Commerce City on Thursday and spoke publicly for the first time about missing the decisive penalty kick in Australia's round-of-16 exit at the World Cup. Herrington's shot against Egypt struck the crossbar after goalkeeper Mostafa Shoubir guessed correctly, ending Australia's tournament run.

Herrington said he was confident going into the kick, having converted penalties consistently in Australia's training sessions, and described the moment as a learning experience rather than a defining failure. His performance drew public praise from Zlatan Ibrahimovic during the FOX broadcast, who called Herrington's willingness to step up "a lot of courage" for an 18-year-old on the world stage.

Herrington made his senior international debut for Australia in March, progressed to earn a spot on the 26-man World Cup roster, and started in Australia's must-win group stage match against Paraguay. His development has been a bright spot for the Rapids, who have watched one of their youngest players compete on the sport's biggest stage in his first MLS season.

stories.headline: Broncos' Nik Bonitto Launches Food Bank Partnership, Eyes Improved Postseason Impact as Camp Opens
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos outside linebacker Nik Bonitto joined Overland High School students Thursday at a Denver Safeway to launch a checkout fundraiser benefiting Food Bank of the Rockies, running statewide at Safeway and Albertsons stores through Aug. 11. The initiative, organized by the Bonitto Family Foundation, funds food pantries and weekend backpack programs for children who lack access to school meals.

Bonitto, coming off back-to-back Pro Bowl selections and 27.5 sacks over the past two years, said he has spent the offseason studying tape from top edge rushers to better handle double teams and chip blocks. He logged a sack and two forced fumbles in the AFC divisional round playoff win over Buffalo and said performing in postseason moments has given him confidence heading into the new season.

Broncos veterans report to training camp Tuesday. Bonitto addressed the uncertainty at outside linebacker opposite him, with Jonathon Cooper excused from minicamp following domestic violence charges to which he has pleaded not guilty. Bonitto praised the depth behind Cooper — including Jonah Elliss, Que Robinson, Drew Sanders and Dondrea Tillman — saying the group is ready to contribute whenever opportunities arise.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: College is just your opinion, but every time you have to prove it with a bunch of papers.

weekendEvents.title: Underground Music Showcase (UMS)
weekendEvents.description: One of Denver's most anticipated summer music festivals moves to its new home in the RiNo Arts District, featuring over 200 performances across multiple outdoor stages and more than a dozen indoor venues. Headliners include 54 Ultra, Slenderbodies, Goldie Boutilier, Angélica Garcia, Fear of Satori, and Fountain of Youth.

weekendEvents.title: Blucifer's First Rodeo Music Festival
weekendEvents.description: An inaugural artist-led music festival taking over South Broadway venues with approximately 170 local bands over three days, including Bluebook, Cheap Perfume, Horse B—c, Honey Blazer, and Los Mocochetes. All-access passes are sold out, but $20 all-ages satellite festival tickets at D3 Arts remain available.

weekendEvents.title: Cheesman Park Art Festival
weekendEvents.description: The 13th annual festival features more than 150 juried fine artists and craftsmen from across the U.S., along with live music and food vendors. Admission is free.

weekendEvents.title: Viva La Isla Del Encanto Festival
weekendEvents.description: A free festival celebrating Puerto Rican culture in the Rockies with live entertainment, cultural performances, authentic Puerto Rican cuisine, local vendors, nonprofit organizations, and children's activities.
weekendEvents.time: Saturday 10 a.m. - 10 p.m.; Sunday 10 a.m. - 6 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Bicentennial Park, 13655 E. Bayaud Ave., Aurora

weekendEvents.title: Dana Day at Larimer Square
weekendEvents.description: A free community celebration honoring the legacy of Dana Crawford, the preservationist behind the revival of Larimer Square, featuring archival photo displays and live jazz sets from the Annie Booth Quartet and Keith Oxman Quartet. Guests who RSVP receive a complimentary glass of champagne.

weekendEvents.title: Summer BBQ Block Party at Branch & Barrel Distilling
weekendEvents.description: A free backyard BBQ-style block party featuring live music, vendors, cold drinks, and food off the grill. Registration is recommended.
weekendEvents.location: Branch & Barrel Distilling, 15353 E. Hinsdale Circle, Centennial

weekendEvents.title: Concert in the Carousel – Flatirons Jazz Orchestra
weekendEvents.description: The 18-piece Flatirons Jazz Orchestra performs at the historic Elitch Gardens carousel pavilion in a free outdoor concert.
weekendEvents.location: Elitch Gardens Carousel Pavilion, 3775 Tennyson St., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Arapahoe County Fair
weekendEvents.description: The Arapahoe County Fair wraps up its run this weekend with axe throwing, carnival rides, a petting zoo, mutton busting, and fried food. General admission is $34.
weekendEvents.location: Arapahoe County Fairgrounds, 25690 E. Quincy Ave., Aurora

weekendEvents.title: Sundays on Santa Fe Gallery Crawl
weekendEvents.description: A free monthly gallery crawl through the Santa Fe Arts District, featuring open galleries on the last Sunday of each month.

weekendEvents.title: Castle Rock Cattle Drive – Douglas County Fair & Rodeo Kickoff
weekendEvents.description: The Town of Castle Rock's Western Heritage Welcome kicks off Douglas County Fair and Rodeo weekend with a cattle drive through downtown Castle Rock and a performance by the Rocky Mountain Indigenous Dancers. The event is free to watch.

weekendEvents.title: Yappy Hour: Dawgs & Dogs
weekendEvents.description: A free monthly outdoor social event featuring live music, drinks, and a dog-friendly atmosphere at the Orchard Town Center's sunken plaza.
weekendEvents.location: OTC Sunken Plaza, 14697 Delaware St., Westminster

topStory.headline: Republican Support for Trump's Economic Policies Erodes as Gas Prices Rise and Tariff Concerns Mount
topStory.summary:
A new Pew Research Center poll released Thursday shows growing skepticism among Republican voters about President Donald Trump's economic policies, with 28 percent of Republicans now saying those policies have made economic conditions worse — up from 18 percent in January 2026. Overall, 60 percent of all respondents said Trump's policies have had a negative effect on the economy, and just 24 percent of all voters described current economic conditions as "excellent" or "good."

Economists and critics have pointed to Trump's tariff policies and the ongoing conflict with Iran as key drivers of economic strain. National gas prices climbed to $4.091 per gallon as of Thursday, according to AAA, driven in part by disruptions to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz following U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran in February. White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers said Trump "remains committed to unleashing American energy dominance, cutting costs, and putting more money back in the pockets of hardworking American families."

Despite the erosion in economic confidence, Trump retains strong overall support within his party. Sixty-nine percent of Republicans said they approve of his job performance, and among self-identified conservative Republicans, his net approval rating stood at plus-54 points. The last time a majority of Pew respondents viewed the economy favorably was January 2020, before the COVID-19 pandemic.

The poll arrives roughly 18 months into Trump's second term and with midterm elections approaching, as Democrats move to make the economy a centerpiece of their campaigns. Polling consistently shows the economy ranks as voters' top concern heading into November, raising the political stakes for the administration's handling of tariffs, energy costs, and the broader fallout from Middle East hostilities.
